title: Dry stone wall basic repair
subtitle: Rebuilding a gapped or tumbled section of dry stone wall to stock-proof standard.
excerpt: A dry stone wall that's lost stones and opened a gap is a stock escape waiting to happen. The repair is straightforward, there's no mortar, no specialist tools, and the principles are simple. Rebuild from the ground up, keep the face vertical, and fill the core before you cap. Here's how.
sourceNotes: Dry Stone Walling Association of Great Britain, Basic Walling Guide. Cobbett, Cottage Economy (1822), Project Gutenberg, field boundary chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: A long, wide stone that spans the full width of the wall from face to face, tying the two outer skins together; placed roughly one-third to half-way up the wall.
The stones placed along the top of a completed dry stone wall, often tilted at an angle, to protect the wall from frost damage and discourage animals from stepping on it.

content.text: A dry stone wall is two outer faces of stone with a rubble core between them. The two faces lean slightly inward toward each other as the wall rises, the batter, and are tied together at intervals by
that span the full wall width. A section that has tumbled is usually a section where the through stones were absent, the core was too light, or the batter was lost.

Carry or roll all fallen stones as close to the repair section as possible. Sort them roughly: large flat base stones, medium face stones, long through stones, small core fill, and coping. You won't have the luxury of picking the perfect stone mid-rebuild if the material is spread out across the field.
Clear back to sound wall on both sides of the gap, even if it means removing a few extra courses. A repair that butts up against a loose section fails again quickly.
Lay the foundation course with the largest, flattest stones, wider than the finished wall face. The base width should be roughly 60,70 cm for a stock-height wall.
Build both faces simultaneously, course by course. Each stone should sit on two stones below it, not bridge a single stone. Fill the core as you go with small rubble, packed tightly.
at half wall height, one every 60,90 cm along the repair section.
Continue building with the batter maintained, hold a wooden batter frame against the face to check the lean as you go. The top of the wall should be 5,10 cm narrower than the base.
: stand them upright and close together along the top course, alternating their lean slightly to interlock them.

attrs.title: Every stone has a best face

content.text: Turn each stone over before placing it. Most field stones have a flat edge and a lumpy edge. The flat edge goes outward on the face; the lumpy edge goes into the core. A wall built with the flat faces outward sheds rain well and looks tight; a wall built haphazardly looks loose and weathers faster.
